speaker: interviewer
text: In looking back in your time in Australia, are you glad that you've come to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say. I love Israel. And I wanted to go to Israel. And I wanted to settle in Israel. But my daughter having two little boys was afraid-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --to let them grow up in Israel. When I was in Israel, they visited me, visit children there. And I told her, there I felt at home. In the beginning, I didn't feel at home. I had to with very hard having a child alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't even make friends because when it came [INAUDIBLE] I had to work at home because all week I worked hospitals, private hospitals, private homes because I could earn more. That flat I bought in '71. When they started to build it with the money, my employer gave me the first $500 in advance because they wanted to keep me. They were not Jewish people. It was a couple of Germans, husband and wife, elderly people. I got out of hospital because I paid too much tax, and I didn't have enough money raise my daughter and her schooling, to pay for everything, all of the bills. And the hospitals didn't pay enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked rather up the paper. And I thought if I have a private-- I've heard from other nurses that privately, one earns more. So I found a position, a German couple, non-Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --in Glen Iris. I remember the very first private position. And because they were Germans, these people never learned English. They couldn't speak English at all. At exam, the first things they asked me if I know English-- know German. I said yes. So they invited me to come. And they were both sick, the husband and wife. The wife was very sick. And they had a niece. The niece was German. The husband was Czechoslovakian, a very nice man. And I think he was a lawyer. And they-- the first interview, I had to visit them. And he said that I'm engaged straight away because he can't speak another language, only German. And they had an Australian nurse whom they couldn't understand. And they want to send her away to engage me. That's what they did. And because they wanted to keep me, they had their housekeeper-- because the woman was bedridden. And they need a special diet. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So-- and another thing is they had day and night nurse. The night nurse, again, Australian. And again, they didn't understand each other. So they sent her away-- both away. And they begged me if I could work day and night with two-hour sleeps. It was a very hard place. I had a room there. But I said, I have a child. And I can't stay away the night. Daytime, yes. She went to school here. And I have to be at home, you see, at night time. They said, they'll pay as much as-- how much I want-- only to mention how much. They were wealthy people. I still remember the street where it was in Glen Iris.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, all right. Bring your daughter here. And she will sleep with you. But to school--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --she said, her niece, she would drive her to school from there where she was at school. I think it was in Caulfield Central, if I do remember back so many years. And so they did. And I stayed night. During the day-- not night time-- during the day, I could sleep two hours. That's how I saved money. That's how I bought my first-- they gave me the first $500 in advance when it was built only. In '71, I moved in. It was end of '70 when they built it. And he went up by car during the day. His wife stayed with them to show it to me. They did-- I still have a letter from him later on. Very nice man. They knew that I'm Jewish. And he went up to me to show me how it is here, how they work here. And I stayed with them 'til she died. And she died only about I think a year later. Then was left a husband, an old man. And he one-- one and a half years I think. And since he-- told me, said in a nice way, they would like to keep me. They were very satisfied with me, even thankful. But because he's alone, his uncle-- an uncle it was-- say put-- they want to put him into nursing home. And it was finished with the job there. But they paid me very nicely. And that's how I saved up money here. [INAUDIBLE] only I paid off the flat in a short time. But it was much cheaper than to get a flat like this now. Oh, it's now about 10 times more.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had to give a message to the young generation of people, particularly people of Jewish background, what message would you like to give them about the lessons of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the Holocaust? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, that's why you interviewing me, isn't it? In other words, what should younger people learn from--

speaker: interviewee
text: What they learn from it? First of all, like everybody else, I have seen it. That's not true. They say that we were slaughtered like sheep. Or so there was in Warsaw, not in Lwow. In large-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --people tried. And they didn't want to be slaughtered. But I know best that even if you-- as I-- I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't say of myself talk like the whole community there. But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't think that you can get character if you don't have it. But to fight, yes. That's a bad thing to do-- to fight. Because I fought my way through. I fought, as I told you, the whole story. And I was never afraid. But where my courage came from, I think it's my mother, I inherited it. What can you do-- tell to people? Never forget it. Never forget it, and to do all possible--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --not to let it happen again. And finally, your idea, or your concept, your feeling of being Jewish, has that changed at all because of what you went through during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. Not at all. I came from-- actually my mother's side, very religious people. Even there was a rabbi there in shohetim. My mother's two brothers were in shohetim. It means they studied in yeshivas for years for it. It's not just a shohet to kill animals. But the way they do kill it, it's not what the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] might say that we killed them the wrong way. And we shouldn't do it. It's better to shoot them. But I know it's not. They know exactly. They studied how to kill an animal that wouldn't suffer. So actually, my mother went from a very religious home. But my mother was unfortunate. She was not religious at all. I don't know. Even she told me, said, often she thinks so deeply that she arrives at a thought, perhaps there is no God at all. Because what she has seen-- very poor people, you know, in these Polish small places. There were some people, Jewish people, beggars.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember that my father, as I said, he didn't go every day to the synagogue. My brother, I remember when he was at home as a young boy when he studied school. He stopped playing the [INAUDIBLE]. You know, the [INAUDIBLE] that they wore here and on the arm. He stopped. He gave it up. And that's because they have seen-- you see, in a small place, you're nearer, like living like in a ghetto. Though it was not-- it was. One place was like a ghetto. Only Jews lived there. And one sat in a lot of misery that my mother said, why does the rich get richer and the poor are even poorer? And my father used to bring home-- when they went on a holiday, he went to the synagogue. On the Shabbat too. But every day, not.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were always beggars that people had to take home for dinner. So instead of when my father took two men only. They used to go from-- travel from city to city, especially before holidays and beg. They just lived on it. That was how they lived. In smaller cities, just begging. And people didn't give them much because there were not a lot of people who had enough for themselves. They had always a lot of children because those times, you couldn't help it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So people with big families couldn't feed their children always, or shoes have for about 10, 12 [INAUDIBLE] up to 12 children even. And my mother always said, look, I question always God. Why is he not helping them? Why is there a murderer that they helped? Bad people, and the same God helped them, but not the poor people. And after the war, I don't know. I was never religious. And my mother, when I got older, she told me this, although she knew a lot-- a lot about religion. She read Tanakh and everything. She knew it by heart. She used to read a lot of books. She wanted to find-- she said maybe Buddhism is better than our religion. Possible, much better. But since, she said, no. All religions are the same. These people on top, like the Vatican, they are very wealthy. Vaticans are the wealthiest in the world. And what are they doing? They don't help their own people. They ask other people to help them. But they're not helping.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you share these--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I was brought up. And after the war, when I've seen those frozen children in that truck, frozen alive-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --so that little bit that was left in me that said is there God or no God, I was sure there was no one. I don't believe it. If you looked-- if you have seen that, those innocent children murdered in such a cruel way, how can you believe that there is a God? Why doesn't he do any miracles like in Egypt? Why doesn't he send-- why didn't he blank the whole German army? So how can you believe? Is there anything else that you liked to say that you might have forgotten or didn't have the opportunity to say about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand the things--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About what you went through? Anything that comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If there is a God, why did he let me survive just from all the family? Why I'm so lucky, the only one, if you call it lucky? Why not my little innocent little brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I always ask myself too. I don't know whom to ask. I asked once. Yes, I asked a rabbi here. He's not alive anymore. So he said to me, the rabbi, you don't question God. Why not? Why not? I just asked, why not? He has-- if He's alive, if He's there alive or not alive, if He's there where He should be, where they say He is, He has seen-- if we are God's children, His beloved children, why did He let that happen to us? And if someone says, oh, he's a nonbeliever. I don't wonder, not at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, I had a great grandmother that was a wife of a rabbi. I never knew her. But my mother told me. She was so very religious. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was nearly crazy religious. She did-- she was thin. She never ate enough. Every second day, she had to-- she stopped eating at all, every second day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She always washed her hands. She didn't use anything because she didn't trust. It was not kosher. Nothing. Even the kosherest kosher was not kosher for her. She used to put on the frying pan wrapped in these nuts. Nuts have a little bit of oil. She used to put on socks on the cat when the cat came in [INAUDIBLE] from outside. She had-- she stood at the door and put the cat-- on the cat's little feet, socks because he brings in from outside not kosher things. So kosher that she was crazy. And when she died, they found on her breast a cross. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she said once-- they didn't know why they found it. They remembered that she said once-- I don't know. Maybe something-- to be sure that she will go to heaven, so not only at Zion to have-- but the cross too. Maybe something in it too. So she wanted to be safe on both sides and wore a cross too. So, so religious. And after all, my mother wasn't religious because those people didn't think only. If they were taught there is a God, you have to fear Him, there is a God. But my mother was a thinker. She thought very deeply. And she has seen what's going on in the world. She said, no I can't believe in it. But she said, don't say it to anybody. I tell it to you only. Because otherwise, people will say that I'm not normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And you too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this photograph please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I found her after the war. A few years after, she was already here and six and a half, seven. I got there, she was seven, and left her when she was two years old, over two years old. And after so many years, I searched. And she was left of course with a Polish family when I was imprisoned. And then-- and they took me for forced labor to Germany. For so many years, we were not in contact at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was-- no, I didn't do that. That photo was taken. I got it from her when she was in-- in Germany where her-- the father who claimed to be her father. And he got it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kassel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell us about these two pictures please? What's the photo on the left? The photo on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearer to us, the one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, this is on the right. Yes, you're right. It's on the left. Yes, on the other side, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about this photo please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when I already got her from Kassel home and I found her. And because before, she couldn't even eat when I came to Kassel, when I already could speak to her, they took her away from that man who said that he is the father. And she showed me she had some-- an orange. She showed it to me. But she didn't eat it. She kept it from months. It was rotten. And she still kept it and didn't eat it. She didn't know even how to eat, how to start eating. And slowly, slowly, I got her back with me to Furth where I worked in the-- in little hospital. I got a room there with her. And I looked after her day and night. And she slowly, slowly started to eat. And got back her appetite. And later, she was-- not on this one, the other one-- she was overweight. And that was later. Here, she will still under, about eight, nine, nearly nine years, under nine. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --her hair grew. She had beautiful hair, fair. And she has beautiful green eyes. And in Germany, she won a competition, child competition. She was the best looking girl of all other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you please tell us about these two photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is here visiting her husband and the little dog. And that dog, she found a sick dog on the street. And again, she got it in like all other sick animals. And she said, no one looks after it. So I have to take it in and nurse it back to health. So she's here with that dog and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one is about the same time. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us please about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in '70s. 1973. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --it is taken. This is our conductor. He's not alive anymore. He died in an accident. [NON-ENGLISH] from [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you heard of him. And it was-- it has a mere choir-- where I sang. Actually, I started that choir. And it began-- I was in a friend's place. She had a piano. And she played. And I just started to sing as she played. And there was someone from the St. Gilbert congregation-- not St. Gilbert, St. Gilbert. Zurich-- Zurich synagogue. He said, why don't you look up-- you can find a few girls who can sing. Can we start a choir? We had only the men's choir. And we want a mixed choir. So it just-- word got spread that we're looking for people singing to form a choir. And how we formed it. And I was a number of years since I was here from '73. It was began much earlier. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that how-- Dziedzice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, tell us about this document. What does it read there in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war-- --in '46-- --for a while, I worked in Fulda it was. But people who were sent on forced labor to Germany, there were thousands of people in Fulda. Everybody wanted to go home to find their families. But mostly, they were non-Jews because Jewish people knew if they want to find any more of their homes or their relatives. But there were non-Jews. So because we didn't have-- I worked there as a nurse. And we had no doctors. And there was-- there were no trains straight after the war. That was in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: 16th of November, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was straight afterwards--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of document is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a few times. This is-- [INAUDIBLE] transport of-- there were thousands and thousands of people back to Europe from Germany-- Germany was in Europe too-- but back to their places of origin where they were born. On the way-- --we had very sick people. So we had to stop and deliver them to hospitals like in Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Romania, on the way to Poland. And this one shows how I arrived with people in Dziedzice in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's that close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was rather-- Dziedzice-- not far from Krakow, more to the east where I had to go in and-- --they gave me this document that I arrived with people from Germany as a transport sister. It's transport [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's straight after the war in '45. I left the woman who saved me in five months, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this document is a document which certifies your work as a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in camp Fulda. That was the first camp established after-- straight after the war. And there were about 8,000 people from all different counties. And they hadn't-- didn't have enough nurses. And there a lot of sick people, under-nourished. A few even died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
